part.text:
# Conflict Resolution in jj

## Background
Jujutsu (`jj`) treats conflicts as first-class objects, meaning a commit with conflicts can be recorded and rebased just like any other commit. You don't have to resolve conflicts immediately during a rebase. Instead, you can resolve them later by editing the file to remove conflict markers, and `jj` will automatically detect the resolution.

## Requirements
1. Rebase the bookmark `feature-b` onto the bookmark `feature-a`.
2. This will result in a conflict in `file.txt`.
3. Resolve the conflict in `file.txt` so that the conflicting line reads `Feature A and Feature B`.
4. Verify the conflict is resolved.

## Implementation
1. Navigate to the project directory: `cd /home/user/myproject`.
2. Rebase `feature-b` onto `feature-a`: `jj rebase -b feature-b -d feature-a`.
3. Open `file.txt` in a text editor.
4. You will see conflict markers (`<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>`). Edit the file to replace the entire conflict block with the text `Feature A and Feature B`.
5. Save the file. `jj` will automatically detect the resolution in the working copy.

## Constraints
- Project path: `/home/user/myproject`
- The final `file.txt` should contain exactly three lines:
  `Line 1`
  `Feature A and Feature B`
  `Line 3`
- The repository must have no remaining conflicts.

# Conflict Resolution in jj

## Background
Jujutsu (`jj`) treats conflicts as first-class objects, meaning a commit with conflicts can be recorded and rebased just like any other commit. You don't have to resolve conflicts immediately during a rebase. Instead, you can resolve them later by editing the file to remove conflict markers, and `jj` will automatically detect the resolution.

## Requirements
1. Rebase the bookmark `feature-b` onto the bookmark `feature-a`.
2. This will result in a conflict in `file.txt`.
3. Resolve the conflict in `file.txt` so that the conflicting line reads `Feature A and Feature B`.
4. Verify the conflict is resolved.

## Implementation
1. Navigate to the project directory: `cd /home/user/myproject`.
2. Rebase `feature-b` onto `feature-a`: `jj rebase -b feature-b -d feature-a`.
3. Open `file.txt` in a text editor.
4. You will see conflict markers (`<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>`). Edit the file to replace the entire conflict block with the text `Feature A and Feature B`.
5. Save the file. `jj` will automatically detect the resolution in the working copy.

## Constraints
- Project path: `/home/user/myproject`
- The final `file.txt` should contain exactly three lines:
  `Line 1`
  `Feature A and Feature B`
  `Line 3`
- The repository must have no remaining conflicts.

Working copy changes:
M file.txt
Working copy  (@) : pxpvvvvq 3ad478e4 feature-b | Feature B
Parent commit (@-): pxxvszlx 3b0993ec main | Initial commit
@  pxpvvvvq test@example.com 2026-04-08 00:30:11 feature-b 3ad478e4
│  Feature B
│ ○  xysmllmu test@example.com 2026-04-08 00:30:11 feature-a 02f7bdd6
├─╯  Feature A
○  pxxvszlx test@example.com 2026-04-08 00:30:11 main 3b0993ec
│  Initial commit
◆  zzzzzzzz root() 00000000

Rebased 1 commits to destination
Working copy  (@) now at: pxpvvvvq 64fdb2a1 feature-b | (conflict) Feature B
Parent commit (@-)      : xysmllmu 02f7bdd6 feature-a | Feature A
Added 0 files, modified 1 files, removed 0 files
Warning: There are unresolved conflicts at these paths:
file.txt    2-sided conflict
New conflicts appeared in 1 commits:
  pxpvvvvq 64fdb2a1 feature-b | (conflict) Feature B
Hint: To resolve the conflicts, start by creating a commit on top of
the conflicted commit:
  jj new pxpvvvvq
Then use `jj resolve`, or edit the conflict markers in the file directly.
Once the conflicts are resolved, you can inspect the result with `jj diff`.
Then run `jj squash` to move the resolution into the conflicted commit.

\\\\\\\        to: xysmllmu 02f7bdd6 "Feature A" (rebase destination)
-Line 2
+Feature A
+++++++ pxpvvvvq 3ad478e4 "Feature B" (rebased revision)
Feature B
>>>>>>> conflict 1 of 1 ends
Line 3
